title: Lesson 336
transcript: Thank you. We've now talked through all the major components within a public key infrastructure. So I've got a really good diagram here that puts all these pieces together and shows how they all work together. And just before we get into this discussion of a public key infrastructure, fundamentally what a public key infrastructure is, is it's all the components that work together to allow us to trust who we're communicating with on the internet. If we didn't have this entire public infrastructure, the internet would still work in the sense that you could send and receive data, but our modern day internet, where we do online banking and shopping and all sorts of stuff, would not work. Because without this entire public key infrastructure, you'd have no way of trusting who you're actually communicating with. You'd have no way that you're actually sending your credit card information to Amazon or providing your banking credentials to your actual bank. The public key infrastructure is what allows us to trust who we're communicating with on the internet. So let's go through and discuss all the major components that are required to create this public key infrastructure. And let's start with Alice here. Alice decides that she wants to communicate with someone else on the internet and she wants them to be able to trust that she is in fact Alice. So the first thing Alice is going to need to do is she's going to have to generate her own public and private key pair. So she generates her own public and private key pair. And now she's going to need to store them securely somewhere, especially the private key. She needs to secure... securely store her private key somewhere and where she does so on her end point device is in what's known as the certificate store the certificate store and this is a very important term to understand for the exam the certificate store is the secure storage location on an end point device like a cell phone or a laptop or a server. It's an endpoint device. And typically, your certificate store, where you're securely storing those keys in that certificate store is usually going to be in your TPM module, your Trusted Platform module. So just remember, the certificate store is where you securely store encryption keys and certificates on an endpoint device. Now, of course, if Alice is going to want to... have anyone trust who they're communicating, like trust that they're communicating with her, she's going to need to send them her public key. But of course, remember, you never just send someone your public key. Instead, what Alice needs to send someone is her digital certificate. So what Alice is going to have to do is go to a certificate authority to get a digital certificate. So Alice sends her public key and a bit of information on herself to a certificate authority. And there is a secure process to send this information. She's not just emailing it. But she securely sends this information to a certificate authority. And the first thing the certificate authority is going to do is what's known as identity proofing. Remember, this is the idea where the CA wants to confirm that Alice, if she says her name is Alice, the CA wants to confirm she is in fact Alice. Now the specific entity within the CA that does this is known as the registration authority. So each certificate authority Komodo, Entrust, GoDaddy, etc. Each of them is going to have their own registration authority, and the RA does the identity proofing. Once the registration authority has proofed Alice's identity, confirmed she is in fact Alice, then the CA will move to the next step of actually issuing the certificate. And remember, which is the entity within the CA that actually issues the certificate? The perfect answer to that is the issuing CA, or perhaps the intermediate CA. That's the CA that actually issues, encrypts Alice's information with the issuing CA's or the intermediate CA's private key. Now, of course, this is all based on the root of trust, the root CA. And remember, this whole entire trust that we have in PKI is on the internet. It is entirely reliant upon the secrecy of the root CA's private key. And so we have this root CA. that has a private key that's entirely offline, because we would never want it to be compromised. Now, going back to the issuance process, because we have all these different certificate authorities, millions of cert... you know, anyone can run their own certificate authority, we need to make sure we have a standard by which we issue certificates to make sure they all follow the same standard. And the standard that is followed for the creation and issuance of digital certificates is known as the X-509 standard. So the certificate authority, the issuing CA is going to generate a certificate based on that X519 standard. And now that Alice has a shiny new certificate, the CA is going to send it back to her and she's going to store it in her certificate store. And now if Alice wants to communicate with anyone on the internet, she's going to send them a copy of her digital certificate. So let's say Alice wants to communicate with Bob over here. She's going to send Bob a copy of her digital certificate. Now, the first thing that our relying party Bob needs to do here is decrypt Alice's digital certificate. What does the relying party, what does Bob need to do that with? He needs the CAs. public key, because remember this digital certificate was at the most basic level encrypted with the CA's private key, therefore it will decrypt with the CA's public key. Now how Bob, so what this means is that Bob or the relying party here needs a copy of the CA's public key. How does a relying party Bob here have that? Well, it's built into his system. Remember, we talked about this. The public key of the big certificate authorities are going to be built into his operating system, especially into his browser, like Chrome and Safari and so forth. So pay really close attention here, okay? I'm showing a dotted line here. showing that the root certificate containing the CA's public key is coming from the certificate authority. But really importantly, the CA is not sending this to Bob across the internet, because then of course we'd be susceptible to man-in-the-middle attacks. And so Bob already has the root CA's certificate built into his system. And that's how he's able to decrypt Alice's digital certificate. Once he decrypts Alice's digital certificate, he needs to check for two things. One, has the certificate expired? The way he's going to check that is just by checking the expiry date on Alice's certificate. That's easy. The next thing he has to check is, has the certificate, has Alice's certificate been revoked? And the only way that he can check to see if a certificate has been revoked is he needs to query the certificate authority. And the two protocols that he can use to do that are CRL and OCSP. So those are the two major protocols you need to know about that a system, a user can use to query the certificate authority to say, hey, has the certificate been revoked? And the entity... within the CA that responds to these revocation requests is the validation authority, the VA. So it's the validation authority that responds to all these checks to see if the all these queries to see if a certificate has been revoked. And the final piece in here is that each certificate authority maintains their own certificate database of all the certificates they've ever issued and which of those might have been revoked. And so just remember, and this is an important distinction, okay? The certificate database is this giant database of all the certificates each CA has ever issued and which have been revoked versus the certificate store is this tiny little storage, secure storage location on an endpoint device. All right, and here's just a summary of that terminology. So remember, the certificate authority is often known as the root of trust because all the trust we have on the internet of who we're communicating with is based on the secrecy of the root CA's private key. They are the root of trust. The registration authority is the entity that does the identity proofing The intermediate or issuing CAs, best answer is the issuing CAs, the entity that actually issues the certificates. You then have the certificate database. This is that large database that each certificate authority maintains. of all the certificates they've ever issued and which have been revoked. And then you have the certificate store. This is that endpoint storage location. So those are the key ones that you should really remember here related to PKI. And just as an important reminder here, the whole point of this PKI is to allow us to trust who we're communicating with on the internet. And yeah, the internet would still sort of work without a PKI, but not the way that we use the internet today. And so without this whole public key infrastructure, we'd have no way of verifying the identities of who we're communicating with. And therefore, we'd have no trust on the internet. Thank you.
